lenses.guidance: vv 1-18's poetry breaks the narrative prose — Israel's first liturgical text. The chiasm structures around v 11's centre: 'who is like you, Yahweh, among the gods?'. Poetry as theology, not decoration.

The Song of the Sea (vv 1-18) is sung again at Revelation 15:3 as 'the song of Moses and the song of the Lamb' — the chapter's worship becomes the canonical archetype of redeemed-people praise across Scripture.

v 3's 'Yahweh is a warrior' is taken up in Revelation 19:11-16 of Christ riding to judgement. The same Lord who fights at the Red Sea is the Christ who fights at the parousia — one warrior, two appearances.

Marah's bitter waters made sweet by a tree (vv 23-25) — the redemptive arc continues post-crossing: deliverance is followed by daily provision crises, each resolved by covenant obedience and divine sweetening.

Miriam takes a tambourine 'in her hand' and 'all the women followed her with tambourines and dancing' (v 20). Worship can be embodied. Today, name God's deliverance with body, not just thought; song is the theology women lead first.
